news_summary: Breakthroughs in boosting immune responses, nerve regeneration, cholesterol reduction, and natural blood pressure control offer promising advances for treating cancer, chronic pain, and cardiovascular health.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées décisives en matière de stimulation de la réponse immunitaire, de régénération nerveuse, de réduction du cholestérol et de régulation naturelle de la tension artérielle ouvrent des perspectives prometteuses pour le traitement du cancer, de la douleur chronique et des maladies cardiovasculaires.
news_summary_es: Los avances en el refuerzo de la respuesta inmunitaria, la regeneración nerviosa, la reducción del colesterol y el control natural de la presión arterial ofrecen perspectivas prometedoras para el tratamiento del cáncer, el dolor crónico y la salud cardiovascular.

articles.title: Scientists supercharge natural killer cells to fight aggressive cancers
articles.summary: Scientists at McGill University have found a way to supercharge the immune system’s natural killer (NK) cells, helping them break through the defenses tumors use to stay alive. By temporarily blocking two proteins, researchers turned these cells into far more effective cancer fighters against difficult cancers like leukemia, glioblastoma, kidney cancer, and triple-negative breast cancer.
articles.content:
Scientists have developed a new way to strengthen the cancer-fighting abilities of natural killer (NK) cells, a type of immune cell that serves as one of the body's first defenses against disease. The strategy could help these cells overcome the protective barriers many tumors use to avoid being destroyed.

Researchers at McGill University's Rosalind & Morris Goodman Cancer Institute, working with the Research Institute of the McGill University Health Centre, discovered that blocking two specific proteins dramatically improves the ability of NK cells to attack cancer.

In preclinical studies, the enhanced immune cells successfully killed human cancer cells from several aggressive cancers, including leukemia, glioblastoma, kidney cancer, and triple-negative breast cancer. The treatment approach also significantly slowed tumor growth in animal models.

"This approach is particularly promising for patients who currently have very few options, when standard treatments have failed," said senior author Michel L. Tremblay, Distinguished James McGill Professor in McGill's Department of Biochemistry and researcher at the Rosalind & Morris Goodman Cancer Institute.

New NK Cell Therapy Avoids Permanent Genetic Changes

Many modern cancer immunotherapies rely on genetic engineering to permanently modify immune cells. While effective in some cases, those changes can carry risks because they are difficult to reverse if unintended side effects occur.

The new method takes a different approach. Instead of permanently altering the cells, researchers used small-molecule drugs to temporarily increase NK cell activity. Because the changes are reversible, scientists believe the strategy may offer a safer and more controllable form of immunotherapy.

The team also says the technique could solve some of the practical challenges that have slowed the broader use of cell-based cancer treatments.

Faster and More Affordable Cancer Immunotherapy

The NK cells used in the study came from donated umbilical cord blood. Scientists at the Cellular Therapy Laboratory, led by Pierre Laneuville and Linda Peltier at the Research Institute of McGill University Health Centre, isolated, cultured, and stored the cells so they could potentially be used to treat multiple patients.

This differs from many existing immunotherapies, which often require doctors to collect and customize each patient's own immune cells before treatment. That process can take weeks and is expensive and complex.

"These NK cells can be ready to use immediately," researchers explained, making the therapy potentially faster and easier to deliver.

"This approach will make immunotherapy at McGill University Health Centre faster, safer and more affordable," added Chu-Han Feng, a research scientist at the Rosalind & Morris Goodman Cancer Institute. "It avoids the complex process of customizing cells and uses readily available drugs to reversibly enhance NK cells' anti-tumor activities."

Future Clinical Trials for Aggressive Leukemia

The research team hopes to eventually test the therapy in human clinical trials. One of the first targets could be acute myeloid leukemia, an aggressive blood cancer with limited treatment options for many patients.

The proposed trials are currently awaiting funding and regulatory approval.

Study Details

The study, "PTPN1/PTPN2 inhibition improves NK cancer therapy by enhancing IL-2 and mitigating TGF𝛃1 response" by Chu-Han Feng et al. and Michel L. Tremblay, was published in EMBO Reports in April 2026.

Funding for the research was provided by the Canadian Institutes of Health Research Foundation, the McGill University Health Centre Foundation, the Jeanne and Jean-Louis Levesque Foundation, the Richard and Edith Strauss Foundation, the Cedars Cancer Foundation, and Genome Canada/Genome Quebec through a GAPP grant.

The researchers also thanked the mothers who volunteered to donate the cord blood used in the study.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in cancer immunotherapy that enhances natural killer cells to fight aggressive cancers more effectively. This advancement has broad potential impact for many patients with difficult-to-treat cancers, offers a safer and more affordable treatment approach, and is supported by detailed research and preclinical success, fulfilling all criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists boost natural killer cells to better fight tough cancers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Natural killer (NK) cells are a part of the immune system that helps protect the body by attacking harmful cells like cancer. However, some cancers find ways to hide from these cells, making treatment difficult.

**What happened** – Researchers at McGill University discovered a method to temporarily boost NK cells by blocking two specific proteins. This change made the cells much better at killing cancer cells from several hard-to-treat cancers, including leukemia and breast cancer. They used drugs instead of permanent genetic changes, making the process safer and easier.

**Impact** – This new approach could help patients who have few treatment options left because it works faster, is less expensive, and avoids complicated procedures. The NK cells come from donated umbilical cord blood, so they can be prepared ahead of time and used quickly, unlike current treatments that require customizing each patient’s cells.

**What's next step** – The team plans to start human trials, especially focusing on a fast-growing blood cancer called acute myeloid leukemia. They are currently waiting for funding and approval to begin these tests.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found a safer and faster way to boost the immune system’s natural killer cells to better attack aggressive cancers using reversible drug treatment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques renforcent les cellules tueuses naturelles pour mieux combattre les cancers agressifs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cellules tueuses naturelles (NK) font partie du système immunitaire et aident à protéger le corps en attaquant les cellules nuisibles comme celles du cancer. Cependant, certains cancers réussissent à se cacher de ces cellules, ce qui rend le traitement difficile.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université McGill ont découvert une méthode pour renforcer temporairement les cellules NK en bloquant deux protéines spécifiques. Ce changement a rendu ces cellules beaucoup plus efficaces pour tuer des cellules cancéreuses de plusieurs cancers difficiles à traiter, comme la leucémie et le cancer du sein. Ils ont utilisé des médicaments au lieu de modifications génétiques permanentes, ce qui rend le procédé plus sûr et plus simple.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle approche pourrait aider les patients avec peu d’options car elle est plus rapide, moins coûteuse, et évite les procédures compliquées. Les cellules NK proviennent du sang de cordon ombilical donné, donc elles peuvent être préparées à l’avance et utilisées rapidement, contrairement aux traitements actuels qui nécessitent de personnaliser les cellules de chaque patient.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit de commencer des essais cliniques sur des humains, en se concentrant particulièrement sur une leucémie aiguë myéloïde, un cancer du sang à croissance rapide. Ils attendent actuellement des financements et des autorisations pour lancer ces tests.

**Une phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé un moyen plus sûr et plus rapide de renforcer les cellules tueuses naturelles du système immunitaire pour mieux attaquer les cancers agressifs grâce à un traitement médicamenteux réversible.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos potencian las células asesinas naturales para combatir mejor los cánceres agresivos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las células asesinas naturales (NK) son parte del sistema inmunológico que ayuda a proteger el cuerpo atacando células dañinas como las del cáncer. Sin embargo, algunos cánceres logran esconderse de estas células, dificultando el tratamiento.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad McGill descubrieron una forma de aumentar temporalmente la actividad de las células NK bloqueando dos proteínas específicas. Esto hizo que las células fueran mucho mejores para matar células cancerosas de varios cánceres difíciles de tratar, como la leucemia y el cáncer de mama. Usaron medicamentos en vez de cambios genéticos permanentes, haciendo el proceso más seguro y sencillo.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo método podría ayudar a pacientes con pocas opciones porque es más rápido, menos costoso y evita procedimientos complicados. Las células NK provienen de sangre de cordón umbilical donada, por lo que pueden prepararse con anticipación y usarse rápidamente, a diferencia de tratamientos actuales que requieren personalizar las células de cada paciente.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea comenzar pruebas en humanos, enfocándose especialmente en una leucemia mieloide aguda, un cáncer de sangre de rápido crecimiento. Actualmente esperan financiamiento y aprobaciones para iniciar estas pruebas.

**Una frase clave** – Científicos encontraron una forma más segura y rápida de potenciar las células asesinas naturales del sistema inmunológico para atacar mejor los cánceres agresivos mediante un tratamiento reversible con medicamentos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of glowing, stylized natural killer cells depicted as radiant golden orbs with dynamic, protective energy waves breaking through dark, textured barriers symbolizing aggressive tumors, surrounded by soft, natural hues of amber and deep blue, conveying strength and hope in a microscopic, abstract cellular landscape.

articles.title: Scientists “recharge” damaged nerves to ease chronic pain
articles.summary: For millions battling chronic nerve pain, even the softest touch can feel agonizing — but scientists may have uncovered a radically new way to stop it at the source. Researchers at Duke University found that damaged nerves can be revived by supplying them with healthy mitochondria, the tiny energy producers inside cells.
articles.content:
Millions of people live with chronic nerve pain that can make even the lightest touch feel intense and unbearable. Scientists have long believed that this kind of pain may begin when mitochondria, the tiny structures that produce energy inside cells, stop working properly in damaged nerves.

Now, researchers at Duke University School of Medicine say restoring healthy mitochondria could offer a completely new way to treat that pain.

In a study published in Nature, the team used both human tissue and mouse models to test whether replenishing mitochondria could help damaged nerve cells recover. The treatment significantly reduced pain linked to diabetic neuropathy and chemotherapy-related nerve damage. In some cases, the relief lasted for up to 48 hours.

Rather than simply blocking pain signals, the researchers believe the approach may address one of the underlying causes of chronic nerve pain by restoring the energy supply nerve cells need to function properly.

"By giving damaged nerves fresh mitochondria -- or helping them make more of their own -- we can reduce inflammation and support healing," said the study's senior author Ru-Rong Ji, PhD, director of the Center for Translational Pain Medicine in the Department of Anesthesiology at Duke School of Medicine. "This approach has the potential to ease pain in a completely new way."

Healthy Mitochondria Helped Nerves Recover

The findings add to growing evidence that cells can transfer mitochondria to one another. Scientists are increasingly viewing this process as a natural support system that may play a role in conditions ranging from obesity and cancer to stroke and chronic pain.

The Duke researchers focused on satellite glial cells, which surround and support sensory neurons. The study uncovered a previously unknown role for these cells. According to the researchers, satellite glial cells appear to pass healthy mitochondria directly into sensory neurons through tiny structures known as tunneling nanotubes.

When this transfer process breaks down, nerve fibers begin to deteriorate, Ji explained. That damage can trigger symptoms such as pain, tingling, and numbness, especially in the hands and feet where nerve fibers extend the farthest.

"By sharing energy reserves, satellite glial cells may help keep neurons out of pain," said Ji, a professor of anesthesiology, neurobiology and cell biology at Duke School of Medicine.

When researchers increased this mitochondrial transfer in mice, pain-related behaviors dropped by as much as 50%.

Scientists Identified a Key Protein Behind the Process

The team also tested a more direct method by injecting isolated mitochondria from both humans and mice into the dorsal root ganglia, clusters of nerve cells that send sensory information to the brain.

The results depended heavily on the quality of the mitochondria. Healthy donor mitochondria reduced pain, while mitochondria taken from people with diabetes produced no benefit.

Researchers also identified a protein called MYO10 as critical for creating the tunneling nanotubes that allow mitochondria to move between cells.

Ji worked alongside lead author Jing Xu, PhD, a research scholar in the Department of Anesthesiology, as well as longtime collaborator Caglu Eroglu, PhD, a Duke professor of cell biology known for her work studying glial cells.

A Potential New Direction for Chronic Pain Treatment

The researchers say more studies are still needed, including high resolution imaging to better understand exactly how the nanotubes deliver mitochondria within living nerve tissue.

Even so, the findings point to a previously overlooked communication system between nerve cells and glial cells that could eventually lead to treatments targeting chronic pain at its source instead of simply masking symptoms.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough from Duke University researchers who discovered a novel method to reduce chronic nerve pain by restoring healthy mitochondria in damaged nerves. This approach addresses the underlying cause of chronic pain rather than just masking symptoms, potentially benefiting millions of people suffering from conditions like diabetic neuropathy and chemotherapy-related nerve damage. The study is detailed, focused on a single topic, and has broad implications for chronic pain treatment.
articles.personality_title: Scientists revive damaged nerves to reduce chronic pain
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Millions of people suffer from chronic nerve pain, where even a light touch can cause strong pain. This pain happens because tiny parts inside nerve cells, called mitochondria, which produce energy, stop working properly.

**What happened** – Researchers at Duke University found a way to help damaged nerves by giving them healthy mitochondria. They tested this on human tissue and mice, especially for pain caused by diabetes and chemotherapy. The treatment reduced pain for up to two days in some cases. Instead of just blocking pain signals, this method helps the nerve cells get the energy they need to heal.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it targets the cause of nerve pain, not just the symptoms. Scientists learned that certain support cells around nerves, called satellite glial cells, pass healthy mitochondria to nerve cells through tiny tubes. When this process stops, nerves get damaged and cause pain. Helping this transfer reduced pain behaviors by up to 50% in mice.

**What’s next step** – The researchers want to study how these tiny tubes work in more detail and test this treatment further. They hope this will lead to new medicines that treat chronic pain by fixing damaged nerves instead of just hiding the pain.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists at Duke University found that giving damaged nerves healthy mitochondria can help reduce chronic pain by restoring nerve cell energy and function.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques restaurent les nerfs endommagés pour réduire la douleur chronique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des millions de personnes souffrent de douleurs nerveuses chroniques, où même un contact léger peut provoquer une douleur intense. Cette douleur survient parce que de petites parties à l’intérieur des cellules nerveuses, appelées mitochondries, qui produisent de l’énergie, cessent de bien fonctionner.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Duke ont trouvé un moyen d’aider les nerfs endommagés en leur fournissant des mitochondries saines. Ils ont testé cette méthode sur des tissus humains et des souris, notamment pour la douleur liée au diabète et à la chimiothérapie. Le traitement a réduit la douleur jusqu’à deux jours dans certains cas. Plutôt que de bloquer simplement les signaux de douleur, cette méthode aide les cellules nerveuses à obtenir l’énergie nécessaire pour guérir.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle s’attaque à la cause de la douleur nerveuse, pas seulement aux symptômes. Les scientifiques ont appris que certaines cellules de soutien autour des nerfs, appelées cellules gliales satellites, transmettent des mitochondries saines aux cellules nerveuses par de minuscules tubes. Quand ce processus s’arrête, les nerfs sont endommagés et provoquent la douleur. Favoriser ce transfert a réduit les comportements liés à la douleur jusqu’à 50 % chez les souris.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent étudier plus en détail le fonctionnement de ces petits tubes et tester davantage ce traitement. Ils espèrent que cela mènera à de nouveaux médicaments qui traitent la douleur chronique en réparant les nerfs endommagés au lieu de masquer la douleur.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des scientifiques de l’Université Duke ont découvert que donner aux nerfs endommagés des mitochondries saines peut aider à réduire la douleur chronique en restaurant l’énergie et le fonctionnement des cellules nerveuses.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos reactivan nervios dañados para aliviar el dolor crónico
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Millones de personas sufren dolor nervioso crónico, donde hasta un toque ligero puede causar un dolor intenso. Este dolor ocurre porque pequeñas partes dentro de las células nerviosas, llamadas mitocondrias, que producen energía, dejan de funcionar bien.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Duke encontraron una forma de ayudar a los nervios dañados al suministrarles mitocondrias saludables. Probaron esto en tejidos humanos y ratones, especialmente para el dolor causado por diabetes y quimioterapia. El tratamiento redujo el dolor hasta por dos días en algunos casos. En lugar de solo bloquear las señales de dolor, este método ayuda a las células nerviosas a obtener la energía que necesitan para sanar.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque ataca la causa del dolor nervioso, no solo los síntomas. Los científicos aprendieron que ciertas células de apoyo alrededor de los nervios, llamadas células gliales satélites, pasan mitocondrias saludables a las células nerviosas a través de tubos diminutos. Cuando este proceso se detiene, los nervios se dañan y causan dolor. Ayudar a este traslado redujo los comportamientos relacionados con el dolor hasta en un 50 % en ratones.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren estudiar con más detalle cómo funcionan estos tubos pequeños y probar más este tratamiento. Esperan que esto lleve a nuevos medicamentos que traten el dolor crónico reparando los nervios dañados en lugar de solo ocultar el dolor.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos de la Universidad de Duke encontraron que dar mitocondrias saludables a nervios dañados puede ayudar a reducir el dolor crónico al restaurar la energía y función de las células nerviosas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of delicate, glowing mitochondria shaped like tiny, radiant energy orbs gently traveling through slender, glowing tunnels connecting stylized nerve fibers and supportive satellite glial cells, symbolizing the transfer of life-giving energy that restores and heals damaged nerves.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden liver switch that cuts harmful cholesterol
articles.summary: Scientists at UT Southwestern have uncovered a surprising new “master switch” that helps control how much cholesterol the liver sends into the bloodstream. The newly identified protein, HELZ2, works by shutting down the genetic instructions needed to produce apoB — a key building block of the cholesterol-carrying particles linked to clogged arteries and heart disease.
articles.content:
Researchers at UT Southwestern Medical Center have identified a protein that acts as a key regulator of how the liver releases cholesterol carrying particles into the bloodstream. The discovery could eventually lead to new treatments for heart disease and fatty liver disease.

The study, published in the American Heart Association journal Circulation, focused on a protein called HELZ2. Scientists found that HELZ2 helps control the activity of apolipoprotein B (APOB), a gene required to produce apoB proteins that form lipoproteins, the particles responsible for transporting cholesterol and fats through the body.

"These particles are a major driver of plaque buildup in the arteries," said senior author Zhao Zhang, Ph.D., Assistant Professor in UT Southwestern's Center for the Genetics of Host Defense and of Internal Medicine. "What we found is that HELZ2 acts as a powerful control point for how many cholesterol-carrying particles ultimately enter the bloodstream."

How HELZ2 Reduces Harmful Cholesterol

The team discovered that HELZ2 works by shortening the lifespan of APOB messenger RNA (mRNA) inside liver cells. Messenger RNA carries the instructions needed for cells to make proteins. When HELZ2 activity rises, the APOB message breaks down more quickly, resulting in lower production of apoB proteins and fewer cholesterol carrying lipoproteins entering the blood.

"Most previous research focused on what happens to apoB after it's already made," said Yiao Jiang, Ph.D., a postdoctoral researcher in the Zhang Lab and study co-author. "What surprised us is that HELZ2 acts much earlier, by controlling how long the apoB 'message' survives before the protein is even produced."

To uncover HELZ2's role, the researchers used a large scale genetic screening system developed by Nobel Prize winner Bruce Beutler, M.D., Director of the Center for the Genetics of Host Defense and Professor of Immunology and Internal Medicine at UT Southwestern. While studying unusual fat buildup in the livers of mice, the scientists identified a gain-of-function mutation that increased HELZ2 activity and reduced the stability of APOB mRNA in the liver.

Lower Blood Cholesterol but More Liver Fat

Mice carrying the HELZ2 mutation produced fewer lipoproteins, including LDL (low-density lipoprotein) cholesterol and triglycerides, in their bloodstream. The animals also showed greater protection against atherosclerosis, the artery clogging disease linked to heart attacks and strokes.

At the same time, however, more fat accumulated in their livers. Mice without the mutation showed the opposite effect, highlighting a delicate balance between cholesterol circulating in the blood and fat stored in the liver.

"We can think of HELZ2 as a kind of dial between the liver and the bloodstream," Dr. Zhang said. "Turning it up lowers cholesterol in the blood but increases liver fat. Turning it down does the reverse. That balance makes HELZ2 especially interesting as a potential therapeutic target."

A Potential Alternative to Statins

Statins remain the most widely prescribed drugs for lowering cholesterol and reducing heart disease risk. But researchers say the HELZ2 discovery points to a completely different way of controlling harmful cholesterol particles.

Instead of targeting cholesterol after it has already been produced, HELZ2 influences the process at the genetic instruction stage before the proteins are even made. Scientists believe carefully adjusting HELZ2 activity could eventually help reduce dangerous cholesterol levels while also offering new strategies for treating fatty liver disease.

"The idea that we can control apoB at the RNA level represents a major shift in how we think about cholesterol regulation," Dr. Zhang said. "It gives us a new molecular lever -- and potentially a new set of tools -- for tackling these conditions."

Dr. Beutler, a Regental Professor, shared the 2011 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for discovering an important family of receptors found on immune cells. He holds the Raymond and Ellen Willie Distinguished Chair in Cancer Research, in Honor of Laverne and Raymond Willie, Sr. Dr. Beutler is also a member of the Harold C. Simmons Comprehensive Cancer Center.

The research was supported by grants from the National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases of the National Institutes of Health (R00DK115766 and R01DK130959).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a protein, HELZ2, that regulates cholesterol production at the genetic level, potentially leading to new treatments for heart disease and fatty liver disease. This breakthrough has broad public health implications due to the widespread impact of cardiovascular diseases and offers a novel therapeutic target beyond current statin treatments. The article provides detailed context on the discovery, mechanisms, and potential benefits, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find liver protein that controls harmful cholesterol levels
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cholesterol is a fat in the blood that can build up in arteries and cause heart disease. The liver releases particles carrying cholesterol into the bloodstream, but how this process is controlled was not fully understood.

**What happened** – Researchers at UT Southwestern discovered a protein called HELZ2 that acts like a switch inside liver cells. HELZ2 controls how much of a gene called APOB is used to make proteins that carry cholesterol in the blood. When HELZ2 is more active, it breaks down the message to make these proteins faster, lowering cholesterol-carrying particles in the blood.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it shows a new way to control cholesterol earlier than before, at the gene message level. Unlike current treatments like statins that work after cholesterol is made, HELZ2 controls how much cholesterol gets made in the first place. It could lead to new medicines that better reduce harmful cholesterol and protect against heart disease.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to learn how to safely adjust HELZ2 activity to lower blood cholesterol without causing too much fat to build up in the liver. This balance is key because in mice, higher HELZ2 lowered blood cholesterol but increased liver fat. Future research will explore ways to use this discovery to treat heart disease and fatty liver disease in people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A newly discovered liver protein, HELZ2, controls cholesterol production by shortening genetic messages, offering a fresh path to lower harmful cholesterol and fight heart disease.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent une protéine du foie qui contrôle le cholestérol nocif
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le cholestérol est une graisse dans le sang qui peut s'accumuler dans les artères et causer des maladies cardiaques. Le foie libère des particules transportant le cholestérol dans le sang, mais le contrôle de ce processus n'était pas complètement compris.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'UT Southwestern ont découvert une protéine appelée HELZ2 qui agit comme un interrupteur dans les cellules du foie. HELZ2 contrôle la quantité d'un gène appelé APOB utilisé pour fabriquer des protéines qui transportent le cholestérol dans le sang. Quand HELZ2 est plus actif, il détruit plus vite le message pour fabriquer ces protéines, réduisant ainsi les particules de cholestérol dans le sang.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre une nouvelle façon de contrôler le cholestérol plus tôt, au niveau du message génétique. Contrairement aux traitements actuels comme les statines qui agissent après la production du cholestérol, HELZ2 contrôle la quantité de cholestérol fabriquée dès le départ. Cela pourrait conduire à de nouveaux médicaments qui réduisent mieux le cholestérol nocif et protègent contre les maladies cardiaques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent apprendre à ajuster en toute sécurité l’activité de HELZ2 pour baisser le cholestérol sanguin sans provoquer trop d’accumulation de graisse dans le foie. Cet équilibre est crucial car chez les souris, une activité plus élevée de HELZ2 baisse le cholestérol sanguin mais augmente la graisse du foie. Les recherches futures exploreront comment utiliser cette découverte pour traiter les maladies cardiaques et le foie gras chez l’humain.

**En résumé** – Une protéine nouvellement découverte dans le foie, HELZ2, contrôle la production de cholestérol en raccourcissant les messages génétiques, offrant une nouvelle voie pour réduire le cholestérol nocif et lutter contre les maladies cardiaques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren proteína hepática que controla el colesterol dañino
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El colesterol es una grasa en la sangre que puede acumularse en las arterias y causar enfermedades del corazón. El hígado libera partículas que llevan colesterol en la sangre, pero no se entendía completamente cómo se controla este proceso.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de UT Southwestern descubrieron una proteína llamada HELZ2 que funciona como un interruptor dentro de las células del hígado. HELZ2 controla cuánto se usa un gen llamado APOB para fabricar proteínas que transportan colesterol en la sangre. Cuando HELZ2 está más activo, destruye más rápido el mensaje para hacer estas proteínas, reduciendo las partículas de colesterol en la sangre.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra una nueva forma de controlar el colesterol más temprano, a nivel del mensaje genético. A diferencia de tratamientos actuales como las estatinas que actúan después de que el colesterol se produce, HELZ2 controla cuánto colesterol se fabrica desde el principio. Esto podría llevar a nuevos medicamentos que reduzcan mejor el colesterol dañino y protejan contra enfermedades cardíacas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren aprender a ajustar de forma segura la actividad de HELZ2 para bajar el colesterol en la sangre sin causar demasiada grasa en el hígado. Este equilibrio es clave porque en ratones, mayor actividad de HELZ2 bajó el colesterol en sangre pero aumentó la grasa en el hígado. Las investigaciones futuras explorarán cómo usar este descubrimiento para tratar enfermedades cardíacas y el hígado graso en humanos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una proteína hepática recién descubierta, HELZ2, controla la producción de colesterol acortando mensajes genéticos, ofreciendo una nueva forma de reducir el colesterol dañino y combatir enfermedades del corazón.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a stylized liver shaped like a glowing control dial, with delicate threads of golden messenger RNA winding into smaller, fading threads symbolizing the HELZ2 protein gently regulating the flow of bright, cholesterol-carrying lipoprotein particles as they move from the liver into a softly illuminated bloodstream river, balanced by subtle wisps of fat gently accumulating within the liver’s contours.

articles.title: Beet juice lowers blood pressure in older adults in just 2 weeks
articles.summary: Drinking nitrate-rich beetroot juice may do more than support heart health — it could actually reshape the bacteria living in the mouth in ways that help lower blood pressure in older adults. In the largest study of its kind, researchers found that older people who drank concentrated beetroot juice twice daily for two weeks experienced noticeable blood pressure reductions, while younger adults did not.
articles.content:
A simple beetroot juice routine may help explain one of the more surprising links in healthy aging: the connection between bacteria in the mouth and blood pressure.

Research from the University of Exeter found that older adults who drank nitrate rich beetroot juice twice a day for two weeks saw their blood pressure fall. The same effect did not appear in younger adults, even though beetroot juice also changed their oral microbiome.

The study, published in Free Radical Biology and Medicine, is the largest of its kind to examine how dietary nitrate affects the mouth bacteria, nitric oxide biology, and blood vessel responses of younger and older adults.

Why the Mouth Matters

Nitrate is found naturally in many vegetables and plays an important role in the body. Beetroot is especially rich in nitrate, but it is not the only option. Spinach, arugula, fennel, celery, and kale are also good dietary sources.

The key step happens before nitrate reaches the bloodstream. Certain bacteria in the mouth help convert nitrate from food into compounds that eventually support the production of nitric oxide. Nitric oxide helps blood vessels relax and function properly, which is important for healthy blood pressure regulation.

When the balance of oral bacteria shifts in the wrong direction, that nitrate to nitric oxide pathway may become less efficient. The Exeter team found evidence that beetroot juice changed the oral microbiome in older adults in a way that appeared to support this pathway.

A Two Week Beetroot Juice Test

The trial included 39 adults under age 30 and 36 adults in their 60s and 70s, recruited through the NIHR Exeter Clinical Research Facility. It was supported by the Exeter Clinical Trials Unit and funded through a BBSRC Industrial Partnership Award.

Participants completed two separate two week phases. In one phase, they drank regular doses of nitrate rich beetroot juice. In the other, they drank a placebo version of the juice with the nitrate removed. A two week "wash out" period separated the phases so the researchers could reset the conditions before testing the next drink.

The team then used bacterial gene sequencing to study which microbes were present in the mouth before and after each condition.

Older Adults Responded Differently

Both age groups showed significant changes in the oral microbiome after drinking nitrate rich beetroot juice. However, the changes were not the same in younger and older participants.

Among older adults, beetroot juice was linked to a notable drop in Prevotella, a group of mouth bacteria that the researchers described as potentially harmful in this context. At the same time, bacteria associated with health benefits, including Neisseria, became more abundant.

The older group also began the study with higher average blood pressure than the younger group. After the nitrate rich beetroot juice phase, their blood pressure fell. That reduction was not seen after the placebo drink, and it was not observed in the younger adults.

The Nitric Oxide Connection

The results point to a possible reason beetroot juice may be especially useful later in life. Older adults tend to produce less nitric oxide as they age, and reduced nitric oxide availability can affect blood vessel function.

Study author Professor Anni Vanhatalo, of the University of Exeter, said: "We know that a nitrate-rich diet has health benefits, and older people produce less of their own nitric oxide as they age. They also tend to have higher blood pressure, which can be linked to cardiovascular complications like heart attack and stroke. Encouraging older adults to consume more nitrate-rich vegetables could have significant long term health benefits. The good news is that if you don't like beetroot, there are many nitrate-rich alternatives like spinach, rocket, fennel, celery and kale."

The findings suggest that beetroot juice may not act only through the nutrients it delivers. It may also work by changing the tiny ecosystem in the mouth that helps unlock those nutrients.

Related Research Adds to the Picture

Follow up work and related studies have continued to strengthen the idea that oral bacteria are central to how nitrate affects the body.

A 2025 randomized, double blind, placebo controlled crossover study of 15 older adults with treated high blood pressure found that four weeks of nitrate rich beetroot juice selectively changed the oral microbiome, increasing Neisseria and decreasing Veillonella, while the intestinal microbiome did not significantly change. The same research program reported that nitrate intake affected nitrate metabolism but did not produce sustained improvements in blood pressure or vascular function in that treated hypertension group, showing that the response may depend on health status, medications, study design, and the bacteria present at baseline.

A 2026 pilot study also highlighted the importance of the mouth in nitrate biology. It found that chlorhexidine, an antiseptic mouthwash, disrupted nitrate processing and reduced gastric nitric oxide synthesis, while dietary nitrate supplementation partly preserved microbial function and nitric oxide related signaling during antiseptic use.

Other work has raised similar questions about antibacterial mouth rinses. A 2025 Scientific Reports study in rats found that a nitrate and antioxidant mouth rinse supported nitrate and nitrite reducing oral bacteria and was associated with lower blood pressure compared with chlorhexidine treatment. Because that study was conducted in animals, the findings cannot be directly applied to people, but they add to the broader evidence that oral bacteria can influence the nitrate pathway.

A Potential Nutrition Strategy for Healthy Aging

Co-author Professor Andy Jones, of the University of Exeter, said: "This study shows that nitrate-rich foods alter the oral microbiome in a way that could result in less inflammation, as well as a lowering of blood pressure in older people. This paves the way for larger studies to explore the influence of lifestyle factors and biological sex in how people respond to dietary nitrate supplementation."

The findings do not mean beetroot juice is a replacement for medication or other proven ways to manage blood pressure. However, they do suggest that nitrate rich vegetables could be a practical addition to a heart healthy lifestyle, particularly for older adults.

They also point to a more personalized future for nutrition. Two people can eat the same nitrate rich foods but respond differently, partly because their oral microbiomes may not process nitrate in the same way.

What Comes Next

The Exeter researchers say larger studies are needed to understand why some people respond more strongly than others. Future research may help reveal how lifestyle, sex, age, oral hygiene habits, and baseline microbiome differences shape the effects of dietary nitrate.

Dr. Lee Beniston FRSB, Associate Director for Industry Partnerships and Collaborative Research and Development at BBSRC, said:

"This research is a great example of how bioscience can help us better understand the complex links between diet, the microbiome and healthy aging. By uncovering how dietary nitrate affects oral bacteria and blood pressure in older adults, the study opens up new opportunities for improving vascular health through nutrition. BBSRC is proud to have supported this innovative partnership between academic researchers and industry to advance knowledge with real-world benefits."

Together, the evidence points to a striking idea: one path to healthier blood vessels may begin not in the heart, but in the mouth.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific study showing that nitrate-rich beetroot juice can lower blood pressure in older adults by positively altering oral bacteria, which has broad implications for cardiovascular health and healthy aging. The study is well-detailed, involves a meaningful sample size, and suggests a practical, accessible dietary intervention with potential large-scale public health benefits.
articles.personality_title: Beetroot juice lowers blood pressure in older adults by changing mouth bacteria
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – High blood pressure is a common problem among older adults and can lead to serious heart issues. Scientists have been studying how eating certain vegetables might help lower blood pressure. One important factor is nitrate, a natural compound found in vegetables like beetroot, which the body uses to make nitric oxide, a chemical that helps blood vessels relax.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Exeter ran a study with 75 people, including younger adults under 30 and older adults in their 60s and 70s. For two weeks, participants drank beetroot juice rich in nitrate twice a day, and for another two weeks, they drank a similar juice without nitrate. The scientists looked at how the juice affected bacteria in their mouths and their blood pressure.

**Impact** – The study found that older adults who drank the nitrate-rich beetroot juice had lower blood pressure after two weeks. This did not happen in younger adults, even though their mouth bacteria changed too. The juice reduced harmful bacteria and increased helpful bacteria in the mouths of older adults. These bacteria help turn nitrate into nitric oxide, which relaxes blood vessels and lowers blood pressure. This shows a new way diet can affect heart health by changing the mouth’s bacteria.

**What's next step** – The researchers want to do bigger studies to understand why some people respond better than others. They will also look at how factors like age, lifestyle, and oral hygiene affect these results. This could lead to personalized nutrition advice to help people manage blood pressure naturally.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Drinking nitrate-rich beetroot juice can lower blood pressure in older adults by changing mouth bacteria that help relax blood vessels.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le jus de betterave réduit la tension artérielle des personnes âgées en modifiant les bactéries de la bouche
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'hypertension est un problème fréquent chez les personnes âgées et peut causer de graves problèmes cardiaques. Les scientifiques étudient comment certains légumes peuvent aider à réduire la tension artérielle. Le nitrate, un composé naturel présent dans les légumes comme la betterave, est important car le corps l'utilise pour fabriquer de l'oxyde nitrique, une substance qui aide à détendre les vaisseaux sanguins.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université d'Exeter ont mené une étude avec 75 personnes, dont des jeunes de moins de 30 ans et des personnes âgées dans la soixantaine et la soixante-dizaine. Pendant deux semaines, les participants ont bu deux fois par jour du jus de betterave riche en nitrate, puis pendant deux semaines un jus similaire sans nitrate. Les scientifiques ont observé l'effet du jus sur les bactéries de la bouche et la tension artérielle.

**Impact** – L'étude a montré que les personnes âgées ayant bu le jus riche en nitrate ont vu leur tension artérielle baisser après deux semaines. Cela n'est pas arrivé chez les jeunes, même si leurs bactéries buccales ont changé aussi. Le jus a réduit les bactéries nuisibles et augmenté les bactéries bénéfiques dans la bouche des personnes âgées. Ces bactéries aident à transformer le nitrate en oxyde nitrique, qui détend les vaisseaux sanguins et baisse la tension. Cela montre une nouvelle façon dont l'alimentation peut agir sur la santé du cœur en modifiant les bactéries de la bouche.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent faire des études plus larges pour comprendre pourquoi certaines personnes réagissent mieux. Ils étudieront aussi comment l'âge, le mode de vie et l'hygiène buccale influencent ces résultats. Cela pourrait permettre de donner des conseils nutritionnels personnalisés pour aider à gérer naturellement la tension artérielle.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Boire du jus de betterave riche en nitrate peut réduire la tension artérielle des personnes âgées en modifiant les bactéries buccales qui aident à détendre les vaisseaux sanguins.
articles.personality_title_es: El jugo de remolacha reduce la presión arterial en adultos mayores al cambiar las bacterias de la boca
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La presión arterial alta es un problema común en adultos mayores y puede causar problemas graves al corazón. Los científicos han estado estudiando cómo ciertos vegetales pueden ayudar a bajar la presión arterial. El nitrato, un compuesto natural que se encuentra en vegetales como la remolacha, es importante porque el cuerpo lo usa para producir óxido nítrico, una sustancia que ayuda a relajar los vasos sanguíneos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Exeter realizaron un estudio con 75 personas, incluyendo adultos jóvenes menores de 30 años y adultos mayores de 60 y 70 años. Durante dos semanas, los participantes bebieron jugo de remolacha rico en nitrato dos veces al día, y durante otras dos semanas bebieron un jugo similar sin nitrato. Los científicos observaron cómo el jugo afectó a las bacterias en la boca y a la presión arterial.

**Impacto** – El estudio encontró que los adultos mayores que bebieron el jugo rico en nitrato tuvieron una reducción en la presión arterial después de dos semanas. Esto no sucedió en los adultos jóvenes, aunque sus bacterias bucales también cambiaron. El jugo redujo bacterias dañinas y aumentó bacterias beneficiosas en la boca de los adultos mayores. Estas bacterias ayudan a convertir el nitrato en óxido nítrico, que relaja los vasos sanguíneos y baja la presión arterial. Esto muestra una nueva forma en que la dieta puede afectar la salud del corazón al cambiar las bacterias de la boca.

**Próximos pasos** – Los investigadores quieren hacer estudios más grandes para entender por qué algunas personas responden mejor que otras. También estudiarán cómo factores como la edad, el estilo de vida y la higiene bucal afectan estos resultados. Esto podría llevar a consejos nutricionales personalizados para ayudar a controlar la presión arterial de forma natural.

**Resumen en una frase** – Beber jugo de remolacha rico en nitrato puede bajar la presión arterial en adultos mayores al cambiar las bacterias bucales que ayudan a relajar los vasos sanguíneos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant beetroot glowing at the center, surrounded by stylized, friendly mouth-shaped silhouettes filled with diverse, colorful bacteria shapes transforming from dull to bright hues, alongside gently flowing red arteries symbolizing lowered blood pressure, all rendered in natural, soft reds, purples, and greens.
